假设所有词词频之和为total（比如1000000）。当进行动态规划分词时，当进行“北”字子运算时，比较的是log(“北京市”->词频 / total) + log("人民政府"->词频 / total) = log("北京市"->词频 * “人民政府"->词频 / total^2)。虽然“北京市”和“人民政府”词频相对高，但由于多除了一个全词词频之和的分母，概率运算的结果实际上是变小了的。据我的理解，实际上分词方法倾向于分割为字典中匹配的尽可能长的词。

另外，此处用log函数而非乘除法直接运算似乎是为了解决浮点数对于此处概率极小时不够精确的情况。

Just to throw my 2 cents in as well. Actually looked into this before. Few issues I ran into:
1. You would need to pregenerate the chain if you wanted it to be fast. It's quite a bit of data actually. Not sure if it would be a good idea to include something like that.
2. If you don't pregenerate, it would be slow. And if you wanted 100s or 1000s of generated text, it could take a while unless you cache the generated chain after the first run.
3. In my findings, I found it to be better to just use a third party library instead. This will keep this library as small as possible and as fast as possible. A lot of people tend  to use this to generate a lot of data and not just one thing here or there, so speed is somewhat critical.

I like the idea of having that, but it seems too computationally intense to place it in this library.

On keywords only, your problem is that what you would like to do:
```python
def __init__(self, n_points, *args, **kwargs)
```
but this disrupts the assignment of the model argument, since that is the first one in the parent class. Right? 

This is kind of a downside to py2 I think, because the standard way to do it would be to break DRY, which is not only silly but could also introduce bugs. So your current solution is beter; pop it from kwargs. The downside to this method is that you cannot read from the function declaration which kwargs are accepted by the method. On top of that, this way you already made it a kwonly argument for all intents and purposes.

So to deal with such issues is precisely what the ``@keywordonly`` decorator is for.
```python
@keywordonly(n_points=100)
def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs)
    n_points = kwargs.pop('n_points')
```
This is readable from the function declaration, because the information on which kwargs there are is where you need it to be. And the decorator guarantees that the pop works.

key2str is a nice clean up developed for situation such as yours, because rather than doing
```python
a = {p.name: p.value for p in self.model.params}
f(**a)
```
you can do
```python
a = {p: p.value for p in self.model.params}
f(**key2str(a))
```
This is not a really big improvement I agree. I just mentioned this as a tip of a way it could be done, but the way you currently did it is off course equivalent. The reason for pointing it out is because in #41 we talked about the issue of when things should have a str as key and when an object. I think we should strive towards objects always, and when you want to call a function with such a dict use ``key2str``. This makes for a clean internal API, I think you'll agree.

As to cleaning up ``__init__``, yes I could have saved some confusing by saying that the setup of all the axis etc is precisely what bugs me. For the rest I agree that those thing belong in init. But setting up axis and figures involves so much boilerplate code that it obfuscates the logic of your init method.

I apologize for being such a pain in the neck about this, and I agree that since this is noted as contrib maybe different rules apply. But at the same time I do want to make sure that everything is as consistent as possible with the core API to make sure this code keeps working in the future.

Hey! :smile: Thanks for getting back. Its good to know your intention is that.  I have not heard this perspective on GPL in python before. I am more familiar with hearing that in terms of LGPL. 

But even with LGPL, there's enough ambiguity (https://twistedmatrix.com/pipermail/twisted-python/2004-May/007944.html). I've spent quite a bit of time trying to dig up info on actual cases of LGPL re: importing via public repos/pip and came up surprisingly dry. I'd rather not open downstream to the uncertainty.  I had a similar conversation at https://github.com/ScottDuckworth/python-anyvcs/issues/32 a few years back.  

The other reason is I want to avoid duplicate effort wherever possible. A lot of the reason its not easy to come up with cases to clarify (L)GPL in python is many averted the issue by picking something permissive.  A lot of it I feel is due to distribution being in source code anyway (no binary blob issue except in extensions, which are often built from source at install time against system headers, etc.) The only other thing it'd bring to the table is reciprocity, [there was actually a case of that a while back](http://www.b-list.org/weblog/2009/jan/18/django-20/), aside from that contributions tend to come back since maintaining a fork/vendorizing is costly.

You will need to install the StandardFirmata or FirmataPlus sketch on your Arduino.
Next install PyMata on the Raspberry Pi. If you want to use it with Python 3, then in a command window type:

```
sudo pip3 install PyMata
```
Below is a typical script to read a potentiometer connected to A2 on the Arduino. You would need to initialize all the other pins in a similar fashion and do reads on the specific pin.

```
import time
from PyMata.pymata import PyMata

# Create a PyMata instance
board = PyMata("/dev/ttyACM0", verbose=True)

# Analog pin
POTENTIOMETER = 2

# Set pin mode
board.set_pin_mode(POTENTIOMETER, board.INPUT, board.ANALOG)

while True:
    analog = board.analog_read(POTENTIOMETER)
    time.sleep(.2)
```

In aware of the problem. In version 2 of ldap3 I added an auto_escape feature that actually performs two different functions: auto encoding and auto escaping. Also both were erroneously applied to all attributes values (where only auto_encoding makes sense). Furthermore escaping were applied to all bytes greater than  0x7f. This is allowed by the RFC buy it's not strictly mandatory for VALID UTF-8 characters.

The escape_filter_chars() function also made two bad assumption: (1) if a slash is present in the filter the text is already escaped, (2) bytes value doesn't need escaping.

This is very far from how the "auto" helpers should work so I'm rewriting this part of the library with the following guidelines:

1. Split auto_encoding from auto_escaping
2. Add a function that check if a text is already escaped
3. Remove both assumption from the escape_filter_chars() function
4. Apply auto_encoding to both the seach filter text and attribute values 
5. Apply auto_escaping only to the search filter text only if it is not already escaped
6. Add the auto_encode parameter to the Connection object (auto_escape is already present)
7. Add an auto_escape parameter to the search() method of the Connection object to override the Connection auto_escape only for the current search.

Just realized there are a few more changes in there I should probably explain that were related more to getting the unit tests to pass than any explicit conversion.

The espeak driver was calling getCurrentVoice immediately after espeak was initialized to get the default voice.  Unfortunately, in my testing, it appears that espeak doesn't actually set a default voice until something is synthesized.  I checked the espeak code and it appears that, if they are asked to synthesize something, and no voice is set, then they load a voice named specifically "default" so I figured it was safe to rely on a voice named "default" being available and used that as the default voice.

The test_say test class had a testStop test.  This test was setting up a listener for a "started-word" event and stopping the engine once it received that event.  In espeak the callback is not called (it seems) until its buffer fills up.  This was causing the stop not to happen until about a second into speaking and the test was occasionally failing because that would be enough time to speak all the words.  I modified the sentence spoken in the unit test to be longer so that it would ensure not all the words were spoken.

The third thing appears to be more of a bug in espeak than a bug in pyttsx.  There are two voices in espeak (on my system at least) which have a capital letter in their name (Farsi and some other voice).  One of the tests iterated through all the voices and tried to set that voice.  The SetVoiceByName function in espeak will take whatever name is passed in and convert it to lowercase.  It then compares this modified name to each available name, unmodified.  As such, it was never able to select the Farsi voice because it simply isn't possible the way espeak is written.  One workaround would be to pass the espeak voice identifier into SetVoiceByName but that would require replacing the pyttsx voice identifier (which is currently set to the espeak voice name) with the espeak voice identifier and I was worried that may impact backwards compatibility so for now I just modified the test to work around this corner case with a comment explaining as much.

				 username_pattern=r"(?:[Uu]ser:|sername|ogin)", pwd_pattern=r"assword",
				 delay_factor=1, max_loops=20):
	"""Telnet login. Can be username/password or just password."""
	delay_factor = self.select_delay_factor(delay_factor)
	time.sleep(1 * delay_factor)

	output = ''
	return_msg = ''
	i = 1
	while i <= max_loops:
		try:
			output = self.read_channel()
			return_msg += output

			# Search for username pattern / send username
			if re.search(username_pattern, output):
				self.write_channel(self.username + self.TELNET_RETURN)
				time.sleep(1 * delay_factor)
				output = self.read_channel()
				return_msg += output

			# Search for password pattern / send password
			if re.search(pwd_pattern, output):
				self.write_channel(self.password + self.TELNET_RETURN)
				time.sleep(.5 * delay_factor)
				output = self.read_channel()
				return_msg += output
				if (re.search(pri_prompt_terminator, output, flags=re.M)
						or re.search(alt_prompt_terminator, output, flags=re.M)):
					return return_msg

			# Check if proper data received
			if (re.search(pri_prompt_terminator, output, flags=re.M)
					or re.search(alt_prompt_terminator, output, flags=re.M)):
				return return_msg

			self.write_channel(self.TELNET_RETURN)
			time.sleep(.5 * delay_factor)
			i += 1
		except EOFError:
			msg = "Telnet login failed: {}".format(self.host)
			self.disconnect()
			raise NetMikoAuthenticationException(msg)

	# Last try to see if we already logged in
	self.write_channel(self.TELNET_RETURN)
	time.sleep(.5 * delay_factor)
	output = self.read_channel()
	return_msg += output
	if (re.search(pri_prompt_terminator, output, flags=re.M)
			or re.search(alt_prompt_terminator, output, flags=re.M)):
		return return_msg

	msg = "Telnet login failed: {}".format(self.host)
	self.disconnect()
	raise NetMikoAuthenticationException(msg)

                # Support direct telnet through terminal server
                if re.search(r"initial configuration dialog\? \[yes/no\]: ", output):
                    self.write_channel("no" + self.TELNET_RETURN)
                    time.sleep(.5 * delay_factor)
                    count = 0
                    while count < 15:
                        output = self.read_channel()
                        return_msg += output
                        if re.search(r"ress RETURN to get started", output):
                            output = ""
                            break
                        time.sleep(2 * delay_factor)
                        count += 1

                # Check for device with no password configured
                if re.search(r"assword required, but none set", output):
                    msg = "Telnet login failed - Password required, but none set: {}".format(
                        self.host)
                    self.disconnect()
                    raise NetMikoAuthenticationException(msg)

                # Check if proper data received
                if (re.search(pri_prompt_terminator, output, flags=re.M)
                        or re.search(alt_prompt_terminator, output, flags=re.M)):
                    return return_msg

                self.write_channel(self.TELNET_RETURN)
                time.sleep(.5 * delay_factor)
                i += 1
            except EOFError:
                msg = "Telnet login failed: {}".format(self.host)
                self.disconnect()
                raise NetMikoAuthenticationException(msg)

        # Last try to see if we already logged in
        self.write_channel(self.TELNET_RETURN)
        time.sleep(.5 * delay_factor)

> I assume something like board[longname] is not supported by all sites, so this other variant gets used instead, right?

The actual 4chan extractor doesn't provide a long name for its boards which is why the short  name is used here. There appear to be long names in FoolFuuka's API responses, though:
```
Keywords for filenames:
-----------------------
board[name]
  Traditional Games
board[shortname]
  tg
```

> What do I have to do?

You would have to set the `directory` value for each site individually (`extractor.4plebs.directory`, `extractor.archivedmoe.directory`, ...), which is probably not what you want to do. Maybe the `-d` cmdline option to overwrite the base-directory is useful here.

This is a pretty big limitation imposed by the config system, but there is not too much that can be done here, I think, except changing the way that these extractors do there config-lookup so that you indeed could, for example, specify `extractor.chan.directory` to set defaults for all of them.

(Also: #18)

You can now use the `L` format specifier to set a replacement if the format field value is too long. For example `{tags:L100/too many tags/}` (https://github.com/mikf/gallery-dl/commit/e0dd8dff5f626a42678a916780b31f0193aef7ca).

Another way to limit a field's size is string slicing: `{tags[0:100]}` (https://github.com/mikf/gallery-dl/commit/8fe9056b16cbbb14b7e94fa92a8c8369cee654a9)

@giampaolo This is actually due to the fact that the psutil whls deployed to pypi are built on Windows Vista or newer (or it seems that way).

The `setup.py` script sets `_WIN32_WINNT` based on the result of `sys.getwindowsversion()` which returns the host OS version, not the target OS. Windows XP does not have the `GetTickCount64` API, which was added in Vista. This causes the above crash on `import psutil`.

Currently, the only way to get psutil to work on Windows XP is to install from source. This can be done with `pip install psutil --no-binary :all:` which will force pip to download the source and run setup.py manually.

However... that requires you to run on Windows XP. If you want to compile on Windows Vista or newer, and be able to run your python application on Windows XP you can't unless you manually modify the setup.py script before installing on your Vista or newer box.

I recommend not having the setup.py file compile based on the host OS, but instead on an extra parameter, like `pip install psutil[winxp]` or `psutil[win32]` and have a lookup table of `_WIN32_WINNT` [values](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/aa383745(v=vs.85).aspx#macros_for_conditional_declarations) values.

        default_route_mtu = ipdb.by_index[ipdb.routes['default']['oif']]['mtu']
        with IPDB(nl=NetNS(some_netns_name)) as ipdb_netns:
            for vid, _ in vlan_addr_map.items():
                ipdb.create(
                    kind='veth',
                    ifname=some_tap_if_name,
                    peer=some_tap_peer_if_name,
                    mtu=some_mtu_value,
                    txqueuelen=some_txqlen_value
                ).commit()
                # Call ovs-vsctl here, in order to add-port and set-port.
                # Call ovs-ofctl here, in order to add-flow.

            for vid, addr_map in vlan_addr_map.items():
                ipdb.by_name[some_tap_if_name].up().commit()

                with ipdb.by_name[some_tap_peer_if_name] as container_if:
                    container_if.net_ns_fd = some_netns_name

                with ipdb_netns.by_name[some_tap_peer_if_name] as container_if:
                    for addr_label, addr in addr_map.items():
                        container_if.add_ip(str(addr))
                    container_if.mtu = some_mtu_value
                    container_if.txqlen = some_txqlen_value
                    container_if.up()
                    container_if.ifname = some_container_if_name

            for dst, route_kwargs in net_config['routes'].items():
                dst = str(dst)
                oif = ipdb_netns.by_name[some_container_if_name]['index']
                if dst in ipdb_netns.routes.keys():
                    with ipdb_netns.routes[str(dst)] as route:
                        route.oif = oif
                        route.metrics = route_kwargs['metrics']
                else:
                    ipdb_netns.routes.add(
                        dst=str(dst),
                        oif=oif,
                        **route_kwargs
                    ).commit()
except Exception as exc:
    with IPDB() as ipdb:
        for vif_cfg in net_config['vlans'].values():
            # Call ovs-ovsctl heare, in order to delete failed port.
            try:
                ipdb.by_name[some_tap_if_name].remove().commit()
            except:
                pass
    try:
        pyroute2.netns.remove(some_netns_name)
    except:
        pass
    raise

Short answer is no. We haven't really done anything with themes so far, not even the preliminary analysis. There does seem to be some complexity there, because a presentation can have multiple themes and I'm not sure exactly what drives that; possibly having multiple slide masters, which can happen when you paste in slides from another presentation (in PowerPoint).

The `CT_OfficeStyleSheet` element class is indeed the root element of a theme part. It came in with the work on NotesSlide because a notes slide master needs a theme and there might not be one present yet, so all it does so far is create a default theme part when there are none.

So you'd be starting pretty much from scratch.

All theme parts will be loaded, it's just that they're `pptx.opc.package.Part` objects instead of something more specific (and more functional). But there are ways to get a reference to that `Part` object and that gets you access to the `.blob`, which you can parse the way `XmlPart` does. From the resulting root element you can use XPath expressions and other `lxml` calls to navigate to the color information.

The quickest way to get to the theme part is probably from the part it's related "from". For the color of a shape on a slide it looks like that would be the slide master. So something like:

```python
from pptx.opc.constants import RELATIONSHIP_TYPE as RT
from pptx.oxml import parse_xml

slide_master = slide.slide_layout.slide_master
slide_master_part = slide_master.part
theme_part = slide_master_part.part_related_by(RT.THEME)

theme = parse_xml(theme_part.blob)  # theme here is an <a:theme> element
srgbClr = theme.xpath('a:themeElements/a:clrScheme/a:accent1/@a:srgbClr')[0]
print(srgbClr)  # --> '93D023' or similar
```

This is aircode, so you might need to tweak it a bit to get it working.

from pyhap.util import generate_mac

from pyhap.accessory import Bridge

def create_bridge():

def main():

    # signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, driver.signal_handler) optional

> Is it possible to run the script 5 minutes before LIVE and it will start when data.LIVE is received ?

No, unless you happen to synchronize the start of your script with the time of the market start minus 5 minutes.

*data.LIVE* is a signal that the data being fed to the strategy is actually real-time and not the product of backfilling or delayed because the server has suffered a disconnection/reconnection cycle.  It is not an indication that the orders will be executed *LIVE*.

If the previous 5 minutes are needed to generate signals from indicators, backfilling is available.

Just as a follow-up in case you are curious (or for other that run into similar issues): the reason why `platform.machine()` returned `None` was that on Windows this function uses environment variables to figure out the information (in particular, it seems to use `PROCESSOR_ARCHITEW6432`, `PROCESSOR_ARCHITECTURE`, and `PROCESSOR_IDENTIFIER`). During the conda-build process, environment variables are not passed on from the surrounding environment by default, to avoid non-reproducible builds that depend on local environments. They have a system for explicitly "white-listing" environment variables, though, passing through the three mentioned variables makes the build work again.

Sorry, I have no idea what's wrong. The debug info reveals that the `ErrorInternalServerError` is thrown on a request for all distinguished folders. It may be that your server does not like one of these distinguished folders. To find out which one, you could try running this:

```python
from exchangelib import Credentials, Account
from exchangelib.folders import WELLKNOWN_FOLDERS, AdminAuditLogs
from exchangelib.errors import ErrorFolderNotFound, ErrorInvalidOperation, ErrorItemNotFound, ErrorNoPublicFolderReplicaAvailable

c = Credentials( 'john@example.com', 'changeme')
a = Account(primary_smtp_address=c.username, credentials=c, autodiscover=True)
for cls in WELLKNOWN_FOLDERS:
    if cls == AdminAuditLogs or not cls.supports_version(a.version):
        continue
    print(cls.__name__, end=': ')
    try:
        cls.get_distinguished(account=a)
        print('exists')
    except (ErrorFolderNotFound, ErrorInvalidOperation, ErrorItemNotFound, ErrorNoPublicFolderReplicaAvailable):
        print('not found')
```

If it throws an error, the last folder it prints is the culprit.

Finally, `account.root.walk()` and `account.root.all()` return a generator which do not generate any requests to the server until you consume then. `list(account.root.walk())` and `list(account.root.all())` would probably throw the same error that you got with `account.contacts`.

Yes, you'd add a new `SubscriptionRequest` service. The service operates on the account level, taking a list of folders, so `Account` would be the natural place to add a utility method (`Account.protocol` is a shortcut to services that are not account-specific, like server timezone info).

It would probably be nice to also have a `Folder.subscribe()` method that is just a thin wrapper for `Account.subscribe()`.

Implementing the other half of subscriptions, i.e. consuming the push notifications, would be nice, but that can wait.

Okay, I think you're best off doing this with the Django ORM.

For each "entry" in your version history, create a `reversion.Revision` model. Each `Revision` needs one or more `Version` models associated with it, where is `Version` is one of the models in that version history entry. For example, for a given admin page, a `Revision` would contain a `Version` for the main model, plus another `Version` for every inline model on the page.

The only difficulty is that the `date_created` field on `Revision` has `auto_now_add` set on it, making a small hack required to override it. This is very annoying, and is being tracked in #482. A pull request is welcome!

``` py
from django.db import transaction
from django.core import serializers
from django.contrib.contenttypes.models import ContentType
from reversion.models import Version, Revision

for legacy_entry in list_of_your_legacy_version_history:
    with transaction.atomic():
        revision = Revision.objects.create(
            user=legacy_entry.user,
            comment=legacy_entry.comment,
        )
        # HACK: Override date_created.
        Revision.objects.filter(pk=revision.pk).update(date_created=legacy_entry.date_created)
        # Assumes that it's possible to get a list of model instances from the legacy entry.
        # You may have to create `serialized_data` manually from the legacy entry, by looking into
        # the format of the django serialization framework.
        for instance in legacy_entry.instances:
            Version.objects.create(
                revision=revision,
                object_id=instance.pk,
                object_id_int=instance.pk,  # Assuming instance.pk is an integer.
                content_type=ContentType.objects.get_for_model(instance),
                format="json",
                serialized_data=serializers.serialize("json", instance),
            )
```

In general,
Default value is evaluated only once. This makes a difference when 
the default is a mutable object such as a dictionary:
```
>>> from alpha_vantage.timeseries import TimeSeries
>>>
>>> ts1 = TimeSeries(key='demo')
>>> ts2 = TimeSeries(key='demo')
# ts1 and ts2 are not identical objects.
>>> ts1 is ts2
False
# but the proxy attribute of both refer to the same default dict which is a mutable object. 
>>> ts1.proxy is ts2.proxy  
True
>>> id(ts1.proxy), id(ts2.proxy)
(2181378373456, 2181378373456)
>>> ts1.proxy['http'] = 'http://10.10.1.10:3128'
>>> ts1.proxy
{'http': 'http://10.10.1.10:3128'}
>>> ts2.proxy
{'http': 'http://10.10.1.10:3128'}
```
For more information:
[why-are-default-values-shared-between-objects](https://docs.python.org/3/faq/programming.html#why-are-default-values-shared-between-objects)

I didn't say you could invalidate tokens, just that Flask-JWT _might_ be more appropriate for your needs. I would say that your requirement of only allowing one token for one user to be valid at any given point in time is quite uncommon. And regardless of if you have a SPA or traditional web app, it would require some sort of unique data about the client to be persisted across requests that you can refer to and manipulate at your own will. This goes against what I would loosely refer to as the "access token pattern", which is a mechanism for authentication against stateless services. Otherwise, most applications allow a user to be "logged in" or use multiple tokens without concern.

Autograd doesn't support assignment into arrays. However, you could rewrite your code like this and even avoid the Python-level loop:

```python
def linspace(start, end, num):
    return start + np.arange(num) * (end-start)/(num-1)
```

Both the original and this version have a divide-by-zero problem when `num` is 1, so you might want to check that with an if statement or assertion.

Oh god you're probably using a statically linked binary aren't you

angr has very limiited support for emulating syscalls. as a result, the line `auto_load_libs=False` is meant to disable the loading of shared libraries to improve tractability - what angr will do is replace all functions imported from a shared library with either a python implementation of the function (a SimProcedure) or a stub that does nothing but return when no appropriate SimProcedure is available. By static-linking, you remove angr's ability to do this automatically. You can still add simprocedures to the appropriate addresses through a process called hooking; read through our docs.

Ok I can confirm that what you are trying to do is not working for me on a `1.12.2` cluster.

From what I see from logs the reason is the following: when I create the second app pulling the image from the first app repo, a fleet unit is created and as in logs by @gergelyke it is trying to start the app with `bash -c 'node app.js'`. This is not working cause the workdir (supposed to be /app) is not set and the container immediately stops. I manually replaced in the fleet unit file `bash -c 'node app.js'` with  `'cd /app/ && node server.js'` and it works.

The reason using `cmd` and not `web` is cause with dockerfiles and docker images deis is supposed to scale the CMD entry. See http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/using_deis/using-dockerfiles/ and http://docs.deis.io/en/latest/using_deis/using-docker-images/. However this seems not to be the case because the image is created with heroku nodejs buildpack so there is not CMD and if you try to `scale cmd=X` the created fleet unit is not running any cmd.

[See here](http://python-for-android.readthedocs.io/en/latest/buildoptions/?highlight=crystax); first download Crystax NDK and set the path to it (p4a/buildozer options). In addition to @glyph's point, there is a lot of automation (for legal departments especially) that check only for LICENSE files given the common usage of that file to include the license under which the software is distributed. Redistributors often look for the license as well as part of their assessing whether to package something or not, so while you might find the file redundant @jaraco, you will be helping end users trying to use setuptools in a corporate environment as well as redistributors to those end-users. See also [this distutils thread](https://mail.python.org/pipermail/distutils-sig/2016-August/029368.html).

This change breaks backwards compatibility in order to accommodate an edge case, and even then it's just for performance reasons, not correctness. Additionally, [the documentation hints that subpackages do get searched](http://setuptools.readthedocs.io/en/latest/setuptools.html?highlight=find_packages#using-find-packages):

```
find_packages(exclude=["*.tests", "*.tests.*", "tests.*", "tests"])
```

Note that the documentation had ```tests.*``` as well as ```tests```. The way I read it, the current release is not aligned with the documented behavior either.

This change also broke the include argument without noting that change in the release notes or documentation. Additionally, projects that want to be able to build on multiple versions of setuptools cannot use include (it doesn't exist on older versions) and so there isn't a way to get find_packages() to find subpackages without also finding unwanted superpackages.

I would like to suggest that this change be reverted, and that setuptools 29 be released with the previous behavior restored.

That's a good question!

In an ideal world, pip wouldn't upgrade setuptools to a version that conflicts with already installed dependencies (and would do dependency resolution) but I don't think that's really something that's doable without making pip way more intelligent and way slower.

It's possible this is just an "unfortunate" combination of two separate "unfortunate" decisions, notably:
- virtualenv upgrades pip / setuptools / wheel by default (new behaviour in 15)
- setuptools unvendored dependencies (new in 34)

At least personally, the first behaviour is alarming and makes it very difficult to have repeatable builds (without scattering `--no-download`, `VIRTUALENV_NO_DOWNLOAD=1` or `PIP_INDEX_URL=... virtualenv` all over our ~thousands of codebases).  The second seems not a problem to me, I rather like that setuptools now acts more like a normal python package (though the pypa tools don't treat it as such: magically upgrading, excluding from freeze, etc.).

In short, big shrugs -- it might just be "unfortunate: won't fix" and that would be ok too!

In summary: ENOPROGRESS

On FreeBSD we don't release pypy using it's release script.  The reasons are:
 * Supporting building multiple pypy versions within one port (i.e. a normal pypy, sandboxed pypy, etc) - not used except for those who do a custom build
 * Allow pypy to fit into a standard $PREFIX structure (i.e. in bin/ include/ and lib/)
 * Tentative plans to make switching from cPython to PyPy as a port's "python" implementation more seamless (i.e. just change pointers to bin, include, lib) [although this change may not be so required for that]

To fix we need to either:
 1) Change the way we install PyPy on FreeBSD, or
 2) Change virtualenv to automatically detect the library paths

This patch attempts to do the later however it wasn't complete and introduced an error.  I no longer have time to work on it.

The corrupt frozen dictionary items indicate that the `runner_cfg` generic is truncated or corrupt in some other way. The source of randomness that you experience could be that the `runner_cfg` is not created deterministically due to Python dictionary implementation where iterating over the keys of the dictionary has no defined order and can be random. This randomness can cause different parts of the runner_cfg to be affected by the truncation causing different test bench behavior. I will push a branch where I remove this randomness and also adds a report directly in test_runner_setup which prints the runner_cfg and its length.

Hi Martin,

Yes, it probably is a good idea to contact David Mastronarde about this. I've checked an older file I had which was written by SerialEM and it's fine, so this looks like a change in behaviour in the new version.

The machine stamp is supposed to contain `0x44 0x44 0x00 0x00` or `0x44 0x41 0x00 0x00` (those are in hex - it's `68 68 0 0` or `68 65 0 0` in decimal).

This is described in the [MRC2014 format spec](http://www.ccpem.ac.uk/mrc_format/mrc2014.php) and the [IMOD documentation](http://bio3d.colorado.edu/imod/doc/mrc_format.txt) (search for 'machst' or 'stamp' for the relevant parts), so the fact that SerialEM is producing files which don't agree with the specification is definitely a concern.

I think we should consider having `rm_rf` raise exceptions when it fails rather than returning True/False.  I didn't change it here since it very likely will break other parts of conda.  Returning True/False encourages patterns like:
```
if rm_rf(directory):
    # yay? I succeeded!
    pass   # what else would I do?
else:
    # um something went wrong, but I'm clueless.
```

findstr /s /m /i /r /c:"timestamp\": [0-9]*\." C:\Users\gsmith\Anaconda2\conda-meta\*.json

I tried doing exactly this through a custom script using 'gsutil', I was using Google Cloud Storage for storage. The problem is that base backups by themselves aren't sufficient for recovery, they need to have the corresponding WAL segments generated during the process of making the backup. The WAL segments required by each base backup can be found out by using the --detail option in the 'backup-list' subcommand. So, while deleting WAL segments we'll have to keep track of each of these segments for each base backup still present.

I ended up setting up a script to copy the the older base backups and the corresponding WAL segments to another directory and then running the 'delete retain 8' command on the main backup location. Wal-e can successfully restore from both locations as long as all ENV variables are set correctly.

Paramiko[0] is a python library that will allow us to SSH to the remote machines running Pulp. 

1. pulp-smash will determine the hostname of the remote machine by parsing the "base_url" attribute of the pulp-smash config. 

2. paramiko will read the ~/.ssh/ssh_config and determine the proper key to use for connecting to the hostname derived in step 1.

3. Utility methods that inspect the state of the remote filesystem will use the credentials from step 2 to make connection to the remote machine. 


[0] http://paramiko-docs.readthedocs.org

Your workaround does not fulfil these criteria as far as I understand.

So there should be some way to specify the `grad_req` without destroying the abstraction that the introduction of Blocks allowed us to have.

> In most situations, however, it is recommended to allocate memory from the Python heap specifically because the latter is under control of the Python memory manager. Another reason for using the Python heap is the desire to inform the Python memory manager about the memory needs of the extension module. Even when the requested memory is used exclusively for internal, highly-specific purposes, delegating all memory requests to the Python memory manager causes the interpreter to have a more accurate image of its memory footprint as a whole. Consequently, under certain circumstances, the Python memory manager may or may not trigger appropriate actions, like garbage collection, memory compaction or other preventive procedures. Note that by using the C library allocator as shown in the previous example, the allocated memory for the I/O buffer escapes completely the Python memory manager.

We want to do a PATCH-call for a payment resource prior to the execution. Consider the following code:
```python
resource = paypalrestsdk.Payment(data)
operations = [ {
     'op': 'add',
     'path': '/transactions/0/invoice_number', 
     'value': 12345
} ]
resource.replace(operations)
```

This leads to the error `TypeError: 'NoneType' object is not callable` - which is not surprising, because [Payment does not inherit from Replace](https://github.com/paypal/PayPal-Python-SDK/blob/master/paypalrestsdk/payments.py#L7). 

In contrast, [BillingPlan for example does inherit from Replace](https://github.com/paypal/PayPal-Python-SDK/blob/master/paypalrestsdk/payments.py#L27). Therefore, a resource.replace call would work here and lead to a PATCH-request.

The solution is simple as far as I can see: Payment needs to inherit from Replace. PATCH-Requests are available with a replace-call.

My thinking behind this PR was the following.
1) If array is 1D then row/column major doesn't matter
2) if array is > 1D and somebody forced query of 1D `unchecked_reference` - then he probably knows what's happening and wants direct vector-like view of multidimensional array.

Always remembering dimensions seems inconvenient  to me, when just quick per-element processing is needed. And such kind of operations (in my practice) on numpy arrays in C++ are dominating for ex. linear algebra (which would be better done with `Eigen`) where row/column major order makes a lot of sense. To be correct, order always makes sense but in real life application you just *have* to postulate row/major common ordering of processed arrays, otherwise at least severe performance issues will arise.
And yes, we can address different elements of matrix depending of ordering, but this is an expected effect.

On the other side `pybind11` is generic library that should provide best possible implementation for every possible case. Here @wjakob note makes sense -- dimension-aware code can just directly use `data()` and `size()` for processing. So added complexity may be of no worth really.

I'll post on the developers list, and if everyone is OK with the current behavior, we can leave it.

Note, even if we conclude that allowing circular references is bad database design, there can still be a use case for allowing pydal reference fields to be created before or without their referenced tables being defined. Suppose for example we have:

    db.define_table('table_c',
        Field('table_a', 'reference a'),
        Field(table_b', 'reference b'),
        [other fields])

where `table_a` and `table_b` are large, complex tables. Now suppose there are some requests where we need the data from `table_c`, but we do not need any data from `table_a` or `table_b`. On such requests, we might want to forego the definitions of the latter two tables for improved efficiency. This is not an example of "wrong" behavior.

In any case, if we decide not to support definitions of fields that reference tables that are not defined, we might also consider preventing that behavior when `lazy=True`. Right now it is somewhat confusing that code that works with `lazy=True` suddenly fails when changing to `lazy=False`. At the very least, we should document this disparity.

can happen with CPU only program and GPU program. There is context switch
and shared ressource (like the memory cache) that if shared between
different jobs can cause a slow down bigger then the number of jobs.

Quick example. If the cache size is 16MB and the working set of a jobs is
12MB(the sizes of the memory region it need to work on at one point). If
you launch 2 jobs, then the working set get to 24M, bigger then the 16M
available. So the each jobs need to read data from the main memory more
frequently then in the past, so it get slow down.

Sorry for not acting on this earlier, in the course of #793 I've now gotten familiar with the code again. A possible problem is that float32 can only losslessly represent integers up to 16777216:
```
>>> int(np.float32(16777217))
16777216
```
The indices in question are over batchsize * width * height, so already for a batch of 128 images of 400 times 400, there would be rounding errors. Inputs will often be smaller than this, but it's not an unrealistic input size either. So we should not defer the integer conversion as much as proposed in this PR! Thank you anyway!

here is the core of the bottleneck version:

```
def bottleneck_block(incoming, num_filters, filter_size, bottleneck_size=None, ConvLayer=lasagne.layers.dnn.Conv2DDNNLayer):
    """
    e.g. incoming ndim 256 -> 64 filters, size (1, 1) -> 64 filters, size (3, 3) -> 256 filters, size (1, 1)
    """
    if bottleneck_size is None:
        bottleneck_size = num_filters / 4
    conv = ConvLayer(incoming, bottleneck_size, 1)
    conv = ConvLayer(conv, bottleneck_size, filter_size, pad='same')
    conv = ConvLayer(conv, num_filters, 1)
    return lasagne.layers.ElemwiseSumLayer([conv, incoming])
```
In between sets of blocks for a given filter size, you change size with a stride 2, like from 256 to 512:

```
network = ConvLayer(network, num_filters=512, filter_size=1, stride=2, nonlinearity=None, b=None)
```

Note, when I first got this up and running with Conv2DLayer it was using CPU on ConvGrad3D. Only after I found a post from Sander about this did I switch to the dnn version and get it going much faster. That's why I put ConvLayer as an option in the function.

According to [wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Xcode) Xcode 6.3 is based on LLVM 3.6 and should therefore except `-fopenmp`, I have no idea why it doesn't. ## On Impact on Big Templates

Sure you can say that someone will hit the 200 limitation anyways but that doesn't mean we should just ignore it because they can hit it. What I want us to think about is how we go to a multiple stack world. If we design features with ignoring these limitations, then we can pigeon hole ourselves into not being able to support the feature in multiple stacks. Migrating stacks isn't simply and you normally hit this down the road when you least expect it. 

I brought this up because customers are already running into these limitations and SAM currently doesn't support nested stacks (this is a current limitation unfortunatly). So in not considering large templates at all, we alienate these customers more. 

@teknogeek0 While many customers probably do have a single function, I don't believe that is the normal. I would assume/believe an overwhelming majority of customers are creating and developing production applications, which require (in most cases) more than one function. We shouldn't be designing features that only look at the small cases, this is an oversight and can lead to 'one way doors'.

I didn't mean to bring this up as, don't do this feature because of large templates. It is meant to make sure we consider these customers in our design so when someone says, I am running into limitations, we can advise them (through docs?) on how to overcome this or maybe go deeper into supporting multiple stacks. Either way, I think the exercise during design time to consider these customers is what we should be doing.

## On Alarms in the same stack

I take back my 'this is a blocker', I didn't consider SAR or CodeStar initially (great call out @teknogeek0). Everyone has a different opinion on what is best here, so we should be able to support both in whatever way that means (seeing a greater and greater case to tackle multiple stack world but think we should still exercise our brains so we don't lock ourselves into something, unknowingly).

I have used SAM in a couple projects and was able to use CW Alarms and dashboards in a separate stack without any problems. Once they are baked into `AWS::Serverless::Function`, you get locked into one template (since they are all the same resources). I recognize this is a separate problem that we might need to solve separately but we should have at least an understanding of this and be mindful when designing new features.

@michaelwittig I don't want to distract from the feature request here. Come ping me on the #sam-dev slack channel if you want to discuss further about blast radius. But for clarity here, they should be isolated yes but you stack will still be updated and you risk something happening to your other resources. When you pull resources out into their own stacks (like ops related things), you don't need to worry about anything. You know that the stack is completely scoped and has nothing to do with your service (or things powering your service).

> So it's certainly ambiguous in the general case, but it's not actually ambiguous here because the file isn't actually valid utf-32LE, which seems like a property that could be used to disambiguate.

Very true.  A simple check we could use to distinguish the two cases would be to see if the byte string that starts with the UTF-32 BOM has a length that's divisible by 4.  If it is, it's UTF-32, and if it isn't, but it is divisible by 2, then it's UTF-16.  If it has an odd number of bytes, we could fall back on whichever prober was ranked highest.

The only thing I don't like about this is that it makes UTF-32 and UTF-16 detection *much* slower, because the entire byte string would need to be read in to determine the length.  Right now we feed in one character at a time and make decisions based on that.  The goal is to examine as little of the string as possible.

We could make things reasonably fast if we didn't care about the case where the string has an odd number of bytes, and just added a new `InputState` called `utf_16_or_32`, and just had `UniversalDetector.feed()` do nothing until had it consumed the whole string if it was in that state.

@mrmachine when objects are created they depend on `django.utils.translation.get_language()` which is the current active language used from django i18n system.

Code executed not in the request/response cycle is not guaranteed to behave as you think if you do not use `translation.activate` or `translation.overwrite`.

As @vdboor said in Django 1.8 translation system is not activated in management commands and `translation.get_language()` will return `None`, which will make it impossible to save the object if you are not activated transaltion system with `translation.activate(settings.LANGUAGE_CODE)` or something you prefer.

@ellisonbg I'm not sure that I completely agree with Conda as a installation default for development.

I find that in many situations that there is a short lag of when things are released in PyPi and are available in Conda. When I use conda, I often have to pip install packages to get the environment correctly versioned (environment.yml files do help ease that pain).

What I'm currently thinking (and we can discuss in person later this week) is:

1. Encourage developers to install the user installation and familiarize themselves with the project BEFORE installing the development (master) version and contributing.
2. Document conda installation of the development version (likely providing an environment.yml file for each project)
2.1 Possibly have dev versions in a docker container.
3. Document the key `pip` steps needed to install a developer environment if not using conda. We can mention that contributors opting for this approach should be proficient with Python2/3 installation, pip, and virtualenvs.

A few comments on toolbars in general, based on work with the Bloomberg designers and Cooper's About Face.

* Cooper claims that "toolbars give experienced users fast access to frequently used functions"
* Both Cooper and the Bloomberg designers clarified that menus (main menus and context menus) tend to be the go to for new users who are learning a UI for the first time. In JupyterLab this is reflected by our mostly ignoring toolbar design right now and instead focusing on menus.
* They also said that good menus should migrate users to keyboard shortcuts as quickly as possible (skipping toolbar usage entirely).
* Bloomberg designers felt that having text along with icon buttons becomes important for actions that have pictures that are not as recognizable. However, Cooper says the most important thing for toolbar icons is that they are memorable, rather than recognizable. IOW, it matters less what the icon is, and more that a user can remember it. He also says that tooltips provide a nice compromise that let's a user discover the purpose of a button, without always showing text.
* Well designed menus can also show icons that match those used on toolbar icon buttons.

Some of these things are probably out of scope for the classic notebook (showing icons and keyboard shortcuts in menus, context menus, etc.).

In light of Cooper's I think the play, stop and restart buttons are memorable enough without text (they have tooltips). 

I agree that "restart and run all" is a good candidate for inclusion in the toolbar. We *should* be encouraging users to verify that notebooks run as they expect. In terms of its visual design, I think we are more likely to design a new custom icon that pick an existing one that has that meaning. I can have @hzarea work up some ideas.

Fair point.  If we can trust the 0 required arguments, then `curry` is able to re-raise actual `TypeError` exceptions.  This may occur in regular use when currying functions that only have keyword arguments.

> Last question: is it really a "feature"? Has this been raised as a possible Python bug?

It's an incomplete feature.  The expectation is that builtins will report their correct signature in future versions of Python.  I view defaulting to 0 as a bug.  IIRC, there have been bug reports about this, and the outlook was for it to be addressed in the future.

On a related note, I would expect builtins developed with Cython to eventually support the protocol that reports the correct signature.  I don't know the status on this.

I guess the `intents` parameter of the parsing API is not very clear at the moment. What it does is more filtering the classification results:
- If you pass a list of intents, then once the classification is made, the winning intent will be the most likely intents which appears in the provided intents list (hence sometimes not the number one intent overall)
- If you pass a single intent, the same logic applies and in this case the specified intent will always be returned so it will force the classification to chose this intent

In both cases, the probabilities computation is computed as it would be without this intents filter.
In your example, the problem is that you added an intent which is very close to an existing one, hence their classification probabilities are split.
If you try to add another intent with very different utterances, you'll see that the probability will stay very close to the one you had before (~65%).

Floating-point constraints are usually translated first into bit-vectors and then into propositional SAT. Conversion terms from/to (non-numeral) reals translate into large non-linear constraints over the reals, which the (new, fast) purely propositional SAT solver can't handle, so it falls back to the (old, slow) SMT core that handles all theories, but is often slower. In the case of these non-linear constraints it's simply not strong enough and it either doesn't terminate or gives up early and returns unknown.

For numerals, we can get rid of those expressions altogether (and I've added another one of those in 59911f78), but for general constraints, there is no efficient method yet.

The particular case of fpToIEEEBV was buggy and I had been working on this since yesterday. You saw this problem because I wasn't done with all my changes yet. Note however that fpToIEEEBV is _not_ a standardized function, and that it has changed semantics _today_, i.e., 

    fpToIEEEBV(fpNaN(Float32()))

is 2139095041 (IEEE-like NaN encoding), _only_ when 

    set_param('rewriter.hi_fp_unspecified', 'true')

This is not SMT-compliant, because it disables all unspecified behaviour for all FP conversion functions (including fp.to_* and to_ieee_bv). Now that the unspecified semantics are implemented I will soon switch that default setting of this option to get SMT-compliant behaviour by default. This means that the conversion of NaN to IEEE BV will return an uninterpreted function with the name `to_ieee_bv_unspecified`. I may further refine that to a partially undefined bit-vector one of the next few days (the exponent bits are supposed to be 1, not undefined. It has the right semantics internally but that's not published via the model evaluation yet).

This is not just a "printing bug", but the objective (aka "handle") returns the wrong data.

A complete executable example can be found [here](https://github.com/sosy-lab/java-smt/blob/086b3e65a741d553788585c4688f7793fba49ad2/src/org/sosy_lab/java_smt/example/OptimizationFormulaWeights.java#L100).

That was indeed a bug, thanks for the report! 

Note that fp.to_ieee_bv is a non-standard, Z3-only function which was used as a hack before the FP standard was finalized, so its use is discouraged. The SMT [FP theory definition](http://smtlib.cs.uiowa.edu/theories-FloatingPoint.shtml) has this to say on this topic:

 :note
 "There is no function for converting from (_ FloatingPoint eb sb) to the
  corresponding IEEE 754-2008 binary format, as a bit vector (_ BitVec m) with 
  m = eb + sb, because (_ NaN eb sb) has multiple, well-defined representations.
  Instead, an encoding of the kind below is recommended, where f is a term
  of sort (_ FloatingPoint eb sb):

   (declare-fun b () (_ BitVec m))
   (assert (= ((_ to_fp eb sb) b) f))
 "

Consider this script:

```
(assert (exists ((p0 Real) (p1 Real) (p2 Real) (p3 Real))
  (forall ((x0 Real) (x1 Real))
    (let ((a!1 (> (+ (* p0 x0 x0)
                     (* (* p1 x0) x1)
                     (* (* p2 x0) x1)
                     (* p3 x1 x1))
                  0.0))
          (a!2 (- (+ (* (- 2.0) p0 x0 x1) (* (* p1 x0) x0)) (* (* p1 x0) x1))))
    (let ((a!3 (- (+ (- a!2 (* p1 x1 x1)) (* (* p2 x0) x0)) (* (* p2 x0) x1))))
    (let ((a!4 (- (+ (- a!3 (* p2 x1 x1)) (* (* 2.0 p3) x0 x1))
                  (* (* 2.0 p3) x1 x1))))
      (=> (or (distinct x0 0.0) (distinct x1 0.0)) (and a!1 (< a!4 0.0)))))))))
(check-sat)
```

z3 says this script is `sat`. That is great and is in line with the expectations.

To get an assignment for the `p` variables, we get rid of the first layer of exists calls, and turn it into:

```
(declare-fun p3 () Real)
(declare-fun p2 () Real)
(declare-fun p1 () Real)
(declare-fun p0 () Real)
(assert (forall ((x0 Real) (x1 Real))
  (let ((a!1 (> (+ (* p0 x0 x0) (* (* p1 x0) x1) (* (* p2 x0) x1) (* p3 x1 x1))
                0.0))
        (a!2 (- (+ (* (- 2.0) p0 x0 x1) (* (* p1 x0) x0)) (* (* p1 x0) x1))))
  (let ((a!3 (- (+ (- a!2 (* p1 x1 x1)) (* (* p2 x0) x0)) (* (* p2 x0) x1))))
  (let ((a!4 (- (+ (- a!3 (* p2 x1 x1)) (* (* 2.0 p3) x0 x1))
                (* (* 2.0 p3) x1 x1))))
    (=> (or (distinct x0 0.0) (distinct x1 0.0)) (and a!1 (< a!4 0.0))))))))
(check-sat)
```

There's no other difference between these scripts, aside from the removal of the `exists` variables and putting them into top-level. (In fact, I got the SMTLib versions from the original Pythons automatically.)

But in this case z3 says `unsat`!

@kootenpv The implicit argument to `dtstart` is the current date, so your rule is implicitly starting *after* 9-1. This will work:

```python
from datetime import datetime    
from dateutil.rrule import HOURLY
from dateutil.rrule import rrule
r = rrule(HOURLY, interval=1, byhour=(10), dtstart=datetime(2016, 9, 1, 10))
r.between(datetime(2016, 9, 1, 1, 0), datetime(2016, 9, 6, 10, 0), inc=True, count=10)
```

However, I would also recommend on additional modification to the rule, which is to use a `DAILY` rule rather than an `HOURLY` rule. `HOURLY` will actually calculate all matching dates, but only yield those matching the `byhour` rule. If you actually want something with a daily interval, so it will probably be ~24x slower than the `DAILY` equivalent:

```python
from dateutil.rrule import DAILY
r = rrule(DAILY, interval=1, byhour=(10), dtstart=datetime(2016, 9, 1, 10))
```

> These stateful validations are run as part of the apply_transaction(...) API but it is my understanding that for the tx relaying only stateless validations (the one that can be done with the class method validate on the transaction class) should be considered. Is that correct?

Right, I don't think we should test against our current state database. So `vm.get_transaction_class().validate(...)` sounds reasonable.

>> What if the transaction is valid on the current head but invalid at the next fork and we are syncing? (probably treat invalid, assuming at least one of the peers has reached the next fork)
> 
> Because, even if we just started fresh from genesis, we wouldn't want to relay any transactions that do not pass ByzantiumTransaction.validate(transaction), no?.

Right, we're saying the same thing, here. While we are syncing (so, far behind peers), we should treat a transaction as valid only if it is valid on the fork that our top peer is on.

Also, after we add a Constantinople VM to the Mainnet config, the node should continue validating against Byzantium, until one of the node's peers have crossed the block number into Constantinople (and maybe only forward transactions to the peers that have crossed over). So I don't think hard-coding will work. We still have to dynamically choose which VM to validate with.

From what I’ve read this should be possible by converting to any of the normal forms and comparing the output. Yes, it is obviously a file format problem. I have a proposal:

Case1: If the soma is a contour I would avoid changing the computation to assume cylinders according to the contour. 
Case2: As Mike says (I think it is already like this)
Case3: If the soma is a three point ensure it is of the expected format:

According to the definition from the same link, the swc "correct" format should be:
1 xs ys zs rs -1
2 1 xs (ys-rs) zs rs 1
3 1 xs (ys+rs) zs rs 1

So in order to avoid confusion I would propose to check if:
1. P1.r == P2.r == P3.r
2. P2.y - P1.y == - P1.r
3. P3.y - P1.y == P1.r
If those conditions are met then we can safely say that the swc format is the expected one and therefore we can proceed to treat the soma as "cylinders".

Yes, this doesn't sound unusual. This library isn't generated like the Java client library, it downloads the wsdl (or retrieves it from cache) and creates the service dynamically. Consequently, this library also doesn't have generated DTOs for API objects like OfflineData. This library uses suds, which does offer some factories to produce these, we discuss this a bit [here](https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib#im-familiar-with-suds-can-i-use-suds-features-with-this-library).

Authenticating to WAMP realms via TLS is definitely kind of the "odd one out" as far as crossing the boundaries between transport and protocol -- it's kind of the "higher" protocol layer delegating auth to the "lower" transport layer. (Or, sort of saying "if-and-only-if the transport is client-authenticated, you get a free pass to the realm X").

So, that's kind of a point in favour of "explicit" -- you're saying, "I trust the TLS auth enough that I don't care about any other authentication for this realm". Whereas, I can certainly see that some people might regard TLS as "bare minimum" (even with client-side certs) to get in the door -- and still want higher-level auth.

(BTW, the "auth stuff" I mention two comments up is more-general than TLS etc...sorry for distracting this ticket :/ )

Ok, I was able to reproduce the issue. It looks that the problem we're facing is caused by some nasty "feature" of a `ld`. I've faced similar issue some time ago with different library.

So in general, by default linker searches for symbols only in those object files within library, that contain yet unresolved dependencies. Therefore, in case `weak` implementations are found by linker earlier than `strong` ones, `libmbedcrypto_a-cc310_mbedtls.o` will not even be processed anymore, as it does not contain other symbols.

There is an option to override this behavior - wrap the library inclusion within `--whole-archive` and `--no-whole-archive`, yet I'm not sure if it is possible to apply it easily with `autotools`.

So another option (more like a workaround) is to add other symbols to `libmbedcrypto_a-cc310_mbedtls.o`, that will prevent linker from dicarding it. Actually, there are some good candidates for that (`cc31_enable`/`cc31_disable`), so I think it would be the simplest solution. I'll propose a PR.

    # Source:
    # http://www.obeythetestinggoat.com/how-to-get-selenium-to-wait-for-page-load-after-a-click.html#comment-1998325550
    import contextlib
    # from selenium.webdriver import Remote
    from selenium.webdriver.support.ui import WebDriverWait
    from selenium.webdriver.support.expected_conditions import staleness_of

    @contextlib.contextmanager
    def wait_for_page_load(timeout=30):
        old_page = b.driver.find_element_by_tag_name('html')
        yield
        WebDriverWait(b.driver, timeout).until(staleness_of(old_page))
    b.wait_for_page_load = wait_for_page_load

In general, widgets and traitlets.HasTraits classes shoudl put the default
value in one of 2 locations:

* Use the `default_value` kw arg to the Trait itself
* Define a `_foo_default` method that returns the default.

This will enable the class to be instantiated with no explicit args if
needed, and the module level overrides will only need to set those values
that are different from the class defaults. I think it is fine to have the
module level API for settings things, but those should not contain the
*defaults* just overrides of the actual class defaults.

The math is actually correct in the existing version. The confusion is because of where the `ann_factor` multiplication occurs. The reason it is correct is because the calculation of the downside risk with the `downside_risk` function returns an annualized value, it does the `* np.sqrt()` in that function. The calculation of the numerator of returns in the `sortino_ratio()` function does not annualize:

`mu = nanmean(adj_returns, axis=0)` so the code annualizes at the end at the return value.
`return sortino * ann_factor'

Agree this is confusing, so I submitted a pull request that just moves the `* ann_factor` to the line that calculates the returns so the math is more obvious:

`mu = nanmean(adj_returns, axis=0) * ann_factor`

OK, well this might be of interest to you...

TVPlayer uses DRM to protect some of their content; Five, 5Star, and all the premium channels. The free channels (some require a login) use HLS adaptive streaming and are not encrypted. 

The channels with DRM use MPEGDASH (mpd) adaptive streaming on the Desktop/Android/Chromecast, which streamlink doesn't support yet, and they use HLS (+DRM) on iPhone. The video presented in the DASH/HLS+DRM streams is encrypted using AES128 with a 16 bytes key (the content key). There are multiple different DRM systems offered as alternatives depending on which platform you are viewing the content from. They offer Widevine, PlayReady, AdobeAcces, and Marlin in the DASH stream and FairPlay in the HLS stream. Depending on which DRM system your player supports one will be chosen. For example in Chrome the player will get the DASH stream and use the Widevine DRM system, and on iPhone the player will get the HLS stream and use the FairPlay DRM system. 

Mostly all of the DRM systems work in the same way; most use a form of [asymmetric encryption](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Public-key_cryptography) with a public and a private component.  They may have a per-user secret stored on the device (individualisation secret) and a site or service specific public key. 

The streaming provider will provide a content key ID, and a challenge token (some opaque blob of data, possibly containing the public site key) and the player will pass this information to the DRM system which will apply some secret process to that data and produce a challenge response token (another opaque blob of data, but presumably it is encrypted and contains the  public component of the individualisation key, details about the platform, etc.). The response token is sent back to streaming provider by the player and, if valid, the provider will send back another opaque block of information containing the content key (encrypted, obfuscated etc.), which the player then passes back to the DRM system. The DRM system will decrypted the content key and store it (possibly encrypted in memory, obfuscated, etc.) and inform the player that it is ready to decrypt content encrypted with that content key (identified by the content key ID). The player then loads the video data from the stream and passes it to the DRM system, which decrypts it and possibly decodes the video data in to raw frames, and then passes that back to player, which in-turn displays it to the user. 

Reverse engineering exactly how that process works is quite complex, because they have specifically designed the system for it to be difficult to reverse engineer. Even if you did work it out, it would be very easy for the DRM provider to change the system a bit and then you'd have to start all over - it would be a game of cat and mouse.

Ah, gotcha. In that case, `pytest-xdist` seems like the only option.

As for the `--dist` param, that's  out-of-scope for this package. Distributing across multiple machines is what `pytest-xdist` is good at. `pytest-parallel` is trying to squeeze all possible performance out of a single machine. If you need to run tests in multiple Python versions on a single machine with `pytest-parallel`, [run pytest with `tox`](https://tox.readthedocs.io/en/latest/example/pytest.html).

I'll keep this issue open as an aspirational feature for multiprocessing in Windows.

Hi,

This issue tracker is for issues with our Python client, so I don't think this question belongs here. You could try again on the Let's Encrypt Community Forum, which is for all Let's Encrypt related questions.

https://community.letsencrypt.org/

If you're just looking for the Let's Encrypt CA intermediate certificate, you can find it at

https://letsencrypt.org/certificates/

We are currently issuing under Let's Encrypt Authority X1.

Hi @Ma-ve, I'm not sure about the Unsupported filesystem layout error; is it possible that you moved anything around in `/etc` compared to your operating system's original web server configuration?

With respect to getting a separate certificate lineage instead of renewing the original one, is it possible that you had any other subjectAltNames as a part of that cert, such as `www.domain`? Currently in order to tell the client that you want to renew an existing cert and replace the symlinks in `live`, you need to specify *each and every name* that's a part of the original cert, so if the original covers `www.domain` as well as `domain`, you would have to say `-d www.domain -d domain`; otherwise the client asks for a cert that only covers `domain`, which is not regarded as a potential successor to the pre-existing larger/more-inclusive certificate.

To put this another way, `-d` is used to specify the complete contents of certs, currently not as a means of selecting or directly referring to a pre-existing cert that may cover that domain among others.

Apparently it is used to test plugins:

> Tests Let's Encrypt plugins against different server configuratons. It is
> assumed that Docker is already installed. If no test types is specified, all
> tests that the plugin supports are performed.

@MarkLalor I invited you to become a ``cmd2`` contributor.  This will give you the ability to create branches within the main cmd2 repo which will make it dramatically easier for other contributors to collaborate with you in the future.

Might be worth checking out some alternative subprocess APIs like [delegator.py](https://github.com/kennethreitz/delegator.py) and [pexpect](https://github.com/pexpect/pexpect) (which relies on [ptyprocess](https://github.com/pexpect/ptyprocess/) for a lot of the heavy lifting).

From a quick skim, my tentative conclusion is that the main advantage of `delegator.py` over `subprocess` is opinionated defaults, some cute but not obviously compelling API ideas like the `block=True` option and `block()` method, and the wrapping of `pexpect` (though this is also a bit dicey because it explicitly wraps the more-portable but not-really-correct-on-Unix no-pty version of pexpect). The pipeline idea is also handy, but it's basically just a tiny bit of sugar (and doesn't even set up proper unix pipelines!), not something we need to worry about in our lowest-level async subprocess api.

And `pexpect` looks *really* cool, but is a bit high level for a basic subprocess spawning API, plus has a really terrifying implementation (lots of code making all kinds of raw POSIX calls directly with workarounds for IRIX and SunOS terminal ioctl weirdnesses, that sort of thing).

Simple way to disable `RootView`: create somewhere file with urls without `RootView`, for example `utils/auth_urls.py`:

```
from djoser.urls.base import urlpatterns as base_urlpatterns
from djoser.urls.jwt import urlpatterns as jwt_urlpatterns

base_urlpatterns.pop()
urlpatterns = base_urlpatterns + jwt_urlpatterns
```

And then include it in your patterns:

```
urlpatterns = [
    path('auth/', include('utils.auth_urls')),
    # ...
]
```

A conditional GET would work, however, since (from my understanding) the CLI is stateless between commands, it would invoke an HTTP request (to compare ETags) for each command which I imagine is more overhead than checking a file's `mtime` (as these changes propose).

Daemonizing could resolve this performance issue and would be a requirement to using a conditional GET. Will briefly look into running daemons on Windows. Another point to consider re: asynchronously updating the cache is, with the way the update policy is written now (update cache on `swagger_spec` access), that the first command run in a batch of commands will most likely run with a stale version of the spec. To avoid this, we would effectively update the cache synchronously, negating the benefits of using a daemon. @kislyuk thoughts?

To clarify, `--test` will never be passed an argument, and indicates that the handler should perform checks for each sub-handler (if any) to see which should be invoked.  This is done as a separate step from invoking them so that the reactive framework can avoid reinvoking handlers as it processes new handlers triggered by states set by previous handlers.  During this phase, the handler can use the reactive CLI (which I just realized is completely omitted from the online docs; you can check out [cli.py](https://github.com/juju-solutions/charms.reactive/blob/master/charms/reactive/cli.py) and [bus.py](https://github.com/juju-solutions/charms.reactive/blob/master/charms/reactive/bus.py) to see what's available due to being decorated with `@cmdline.subcommand()`, until I get the docs fixed) to access the state system and perform various checks to determine what should run.  The handler should then exit 1 to indicate that it should not be called again with `--invoke`, or exit 0 with optional output to indicate that it *should* be called again with `--invoke` (and any output passed through).

If called with `--invoke`, the handler need not do any checks, and should just execute the handler function(s).  If it contains multiple handlers, then the output / argument pass-through can be used to track which ones, specifically.

The possibilities with TravisCI don't end here. Right now we have a job structure with one [build](https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/for-beginners/#Builds%2C-Jobs%2C-Stages-and-Phases) and two [deployments](https://docs.travis-ci.com/user/deployment/#Deploying-to-Multiple-Providers) (see the`.travis.yml` file to the repo for the full description of the `jobs`):

- A deployment of the documentation directly to the `gh-pages` branch.

- A deployment for `pypi`, triggered with a new release. That in practice is done by tag creation  (``git tag 1.0 -m "New tag"``)  and push (``git push --tags``) at any point.
Notice that there are some rules to repspect: ``__version__`` and the number of the tag need to correspond, numbering needs to be update monotonously, and one tag can be set only once and not modified.

I recommend for the future we use Travis for further interesting automations:
- Testing. Of course, this is what TravisCI is mainly meant mainly for...
- Automatically modify our conda recipees  on both our [conda-forge package](https://github.com/gioelelm/loompy-feedstock) and [bioconda package](https://github.com/gioelelm/bioconda-recipes) (yes, we have two actually). 

(Just for future reference: since we will have the updated version on pip this will imply just few steps (1) Running a script that changes 2 lines in the ``meta.yml`` file: namely the version number and the sha256 of the pypi `tar.gz` (2) committing to the forked repo (3) Making a new pull request to the master repo. (4) Wait that conda-forge and bioconda do their magic

We recently made a simple field for our code to make it work with django-polymorphic. I'm a big fan of a more explicit approach with this field than doing lots of magic in `ResourceRelatedField`. 

The fundamental reason for this to work is that we need to get the model name based on the object ID with the queryset. That will make the type verification work.

```Python
from rest_framework_json_api.exceptions import Conflict
from rest_framework_json_api.relations import ResourceRelatedField
from rest_framework_json_api.utils import get_resource_type_from_instance


class PolymorphicRelatedField(ResourceRelatedField):
    """Polymorphic Related Field.

    A field to ignore failed type verification in
    `ResourceRelatedField.to_internal_value`. Rest Framework JSON API does not
    support Django-polymorphic yet. Its type verification is based on
    retrieving resource name from `queryset.model` on the field. In our case
    that will always return the base model name, which will fail for sub models.
    """

    def to_internal_value(self, data):
        """Override to ignore `Conflict` exception.

        Since the resource name is retrieved from `queryset.model` on the
        field, we make sure it's ignored.
        """

        try:
            return super().to_internal_value(data)

        except Conflict:
            obj = self.queryset.filter(id=data['id']).first()
            if data['type'] != get_resource_type_from_instance(obj):
                raise

            # Luckily the Conflict exception is raised at the end so we can
            # explicitly call super on `ResourceRelatedField`, which is the
            # `PrimaryKeyRelatedField`.
            return super(ResourceRelatedField, self).to_internal_value(
                data['id'])
```

`to_representation()` will just work out of the box because django-polymorphic returns the correct sub type of the model. What do you think?

Great question. The underlying slider (jqueryui slider) doesn't have an option for that, but perhaps you could do it for now by listening to changes on the value, and reset that value to enforce your minimum distance.

```python
from ipywidgets import IntRangeSlider
x = IntRangeSlider()
def enforce_gap(change):
    gap = 10
    min, max = change.new
    oldmin, oldmax = change.old
    if (max-min) < gap:
        if oldmin == min:
            # max changed
            max = min + gap
        else:
            min = max - gap
    x.value = (min, max)
x.observe(enforce_gap, 'value')
x
```

That's true @alimanfoo, it is a variant on the first option. The argument is that for renaming the classes implementing the JSON and MsgPack codecs to something approximately the same as the old classes is that

1. All old code with continue to work. People who currently use the MsgPack encoder without problems can continue to use it.
2. In the case of MessagePack, new users will only ever see this codec documented, and don't need to know about MsgPack. I think renaming it to MessagePack is much better than MsgPack2 or whatever, as this will be confusing to new users of the API. (It's less clear for JSON->Json)

I don't think the IDs make any difference; we can call them json2, msgpack2 or whatever. Users will never see them, so the won't be confused by this. This strategy would give full forwards and backwards compatibility, which should be the aim I think.

Indeed, that makes the fix actually a hack that might make things worse for some. GitPython will now emit a log message, but not behave correctly. Maybe using git directly is advised in this case (e.g. `repo.git.submodule('update', '--init', '--recursive')`.
The reason one would want to use GitPython's submodule implementation is only if one wants to have smart behaviour, that is to automatically remove submodules when they have been removed, with support for changing URLs on the fly to existing submodules.

`to_latest_revision=True` does not affect the handling of the branch name `.`, which is interpreted literally at all times.

Interestingly, during the inception of the submodule support of GitPython, I added the 'branch' feature myself as it was one of my requirements to make submodules useful.

I changed your script bellow, let me know how it works for you:

```
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

from imblearn.under_sampling import RandomUnderSampler

# create test data
x = np.array([['aaa'] * 100, ['bbb'] * 100]).T
x1 = pd.DataFrame(x, columns=list("ab"), index=range(1000, 1100))
# adding numeric column
x1['n'] = np.arange(x1.shape[0])

y = np.array([0] * 10 + [1] * 90)

print x1

###### numpy test #####
rus = RandomUnderSampler()
x_res, y_res = rus.fit_sample(x, y)

###### pandas test #####
# convert pandas to numpy
x1 = x1.reset_index()
col_names = x1.columns
col_types = x1.dtypes

x1 = x1.values.astype("U")

rus = RandomUnderSampler()
x1_res, y1_res = rus.fit_sample(x1, y)

# convert back to pandas
x1_res = pd.DataFrame(x1_res)

x1_res.columns = col_names
for col_name, col_type in zip(col_names, col_types):
    x1_res[col_name] = x1_res[col_name].astype(col_type)

print x1_res
print x1_res.dtypes
```
